claim: At a stacking fault energy below 12 mJ m^{-2}, the transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP) mechanism dominates in Fe–Mn–Al–C steels, involving austenite-to-martensite phase transformation during deformation.
evidence_text: At a low SFE (below 12 mJ m^{-2}), the transformation-induced plasticity mechanism (TRIP) prevails, which involves the phase transformation of austenite into martensite during deformation, resulting in a maximum level of mechanical properties with moderate ductility.

claim: At a stacking fault energy in the range of 12–35 mJ m^{-2}, the deformation mechanism in Fe–Mn–Al–C steels changes from TRIP to twin-induced plasticity (TWIP).
evidence_text: At SFE in the range of 12–35 mJ m^{-2}, the TRIP mechanism changes to the twin-induced plasticity mechanism (TWIP).

claim: At a stacking fault energy of 60–100 mJ m^{-2}, the microband-induced plasticity (MBIP) mechanism operates in Fe–Mn–Al–C steels, where dislocations glide in planar arrays without cross-slip, and short-range ordering clusters act as nuclei for κ-carbides, creating slip bands with high strain hardening rates.
evidence_text: At an SFE of 60–100 mJ m^{-2}, dislocations glide along one or a system of parallel planes without cross-slip or wavy slip. Some researchers[12–15] explain this gliding mechanism by the presence of short-range ordering clusters, which act as nuclei for κ-carbides. Primary dislocations passing through such clusters, cut them, and create an easy path for other dislocations. In this way, slip bands with high rates of strain hardening are formed, and the mechanism itself is called the microband-induced plasticity mechanism (MBIP).

claim: Lightweight Fe–Mn–Al–C steels are classified into multiphase steels with Mn 5–12 wt% and C ≤ 0.8 wt% and austenitic steels with Mn > 12 wt% and C 0.8–1.2 wt%.
evidence_text: Multiphase Fe-Mn-Al-C steels Mn: 5-12 wt.% C: ≤ 0.8 wt.% and Austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels Mn: > 12 wt.% C: 0.8-1.2 wt.%

claim: Multiphase Fe–Mn–Al–C steels exhibit strength of 800–1200 MPa and elongation of 30–60%, while austenitic Fe–Mn–Al–C steels exhibit strength of 600–1500 MPa and elongation <60%.
evidence_text: strength: 800-1200 MPa elongation: 30-60% ... strength: 600-1500 MPa elongation: < 60%

claim: Multiphase Fe-Mn-Al-C steels are used for automotive structural components and reinforcements, while austenitic steels are used for automotive structural components and military applications.
evidence_text: automotive structural components reinforcements ... automotive structural component, military

claim: Steels containing less than 5 wt% manganese and very low carbon can exhibit a fully ferritic structure with disordered FeAl (A2), ordered FeAl (B2), and ordered Fe3Al (DO3) phases at room temperature, and are not promising due to much lower mechanical properties.[19]
evidence_text: Steels containing less than 5 wt% manganese and very low carbon can exhibit a fully ferritic structure with a disordered phase FeAl (A2), an ordered phase FeAl (B2), and an ordered phase Fe3Al (DO3) at room temperature and are not promising for use due to much lower mechanical properties.[19]

claim: With a manganese content of 5–12%, the typical phase transformations during steel solidification follow the sequence η → E → C', leading to a final duplex structure composed of austenite and ferrite.
evidence_text: With a manganese content of 5–12%, the typical phase transformations during steel solidification follow the sequence η → E ⟶ C', leading to a final duplex structure composed of austenite and ferrite.

claim: Figure 3 presents calculated phase diagrams of Fe–20Mn–Al–C alloys at 900 °C and 1200 °C, showing phase fields as functions of Al and C content.[25]
evidence_text: Figure 3. Calculated phase diagrams of Fe–20Mn–Al–C alloys at a) 900°C and b) 1200°C.[25]

claim: Al additions (1–2 wt%) significantly enhance resistance to hydrogen-induced embrittlement in high-Mn TWIP steels, by increasing SFE and reducing hydrogen diffusivity thus suppressing intergranular and deformation twin-assisted crack initiation.[29]
evidence_text: Moreover, recent studies have confirmed that Al additions (1–2 wt%) significantly enhance resistance to hydrogen-induced embrittlement in high-Mn TWIP steels, by increasing SFE and reducing hydrogen diffusivity thus suppressing intergranular and deformation twin-assisted crack initiation.[29]

claim: Higher Al content (10–12 wt%) promotes early formation of coherent nanoscale κ-carbides during aging, improving yield strength with minimal loss in ductility (e.g., achieving ≈1.15 GPa and ≈50% elongation) at optimized aging conditions (≈550 °C) in Fe–28Mn–9Al–1C steels.[5]
evidence_text: Additionally, higher Al content (10–12 wt%) promotes early formation of coherent nanoscale κ-carbides during aging, improving yield strength with minimal loss in ductility (e.g., achieving ≈1.15 GPa and ≈50% elongation) at optimized aging conditions (≈550 °C) in Fe–28Mn–9Al–1C steels.[5]

claim: Al increases the temperature of Ae3 and expands the temperature range for intercritical annealing, which reduces temperature sensitivity; an increase in Ae3 allows for a higher intercritical annealing temperature, contributing to faster diffusion and reduction in annealing time.[28]
evidence_text: Al increases the temperature of Ae3 and, accordingly, expands the temperature range for intercritical annealing, which makes it possible to reduce the temperature sensitivity of the alloy during annealing. An increase in the temperature of Ae3 with the addition of Al allows for an increase in the intercritical annealing temperature, which in turn contributes to faster diffusion and a reduction in the annealing time itself.[28]

claim: Aluminum addition increases Ae3 temperature, expands the intercritical annealing temperature range, reduces temperature sensitivity of the alloy, and allows higher annealing temperatures for faster diffusion and shorter annealing time.
evidence_text: Al increases the temperature of Ae3 and, accordingly, expands the temperature range for intercritical annealing, which makes it possible to reduce the temperature sensitivity of the alloy during annealing. An increase in the temperature of Ae3 with the addition of Al allows for an increase in the intercritical annealing temperature, which in turn contributes to faster diffusion and a reduction in the annealing time itself.

claim: Carbon strongly reduces the Ae3 temperature, significantly increases stacking fault energy (SFE), and reduces the onset temperature of martensitic transformation.
evidence_text: Carbon has the strongest effect on reducing the temperature of Ae3, significantly increases SFE, and reduces the onset temperature of martensitic transformation.

claim: Fe-Mn-Al-C steels can be divided into fully austenitic steels (12-30 wt% Mn, 5-12 wt% Al, 0.6-1.2 wt% C) and duplex steels based on austenite (5-30 wt% Mn, 5-10 wt% Al, 0.4-0.7 wt% C).
evidence_text: Fe–Mn–Al–C steels can be divided into fully austenitic steels, which contain 12 to 30 wt% Mn, 5 to 12 wt% Al, and 0.6 to 1.2 wt% C to achieve stability in the austenite structure at room temperature,[6,8] and duplex steels based on austenite, which contain 5–30 wt% Mn, 5–10 wt% Al, and 0.4–0.7 wt% C.

claim: κ-carbides have a perovskite crystal structure denoted as E21 with stoichiometric formula (Fe,Mn)3AlC, but a metastable phase (Fe,Mn)3AlCx (x<1) has been confirmed where Mn atoms substitute for Al atoms, causing crystal lattice distortion.
evidence_text: These κ-carbides have a perovskite crystal structure, denoted as E21, with the stoichiometric formula (Fe,Mn)3AlC.[38] However, a metastable phase with the formula (Fe,Mn)3AlCx (x<1) has also been experimentally confirmed,[39] where some Mn atoms substitute for Al atoms, causing a certain distortion of the crystal lattice.[40]

claim: Intragranular κ-carbide (κ'-carbide) is formed by spinodal decomposition, leading to homogeneous distribution in the γ-matrix with high dispersion of 2-3 nm; however, some works dispute the spinodal character due to crystal lattice mismatch, proposing that κ-carbides with simple cubic lattice form by coagulation of intermediate metastable L1_2 phase enriched in carbon.
evidence_text: Intragranular κ-carbide, often referred to as κ’-carbide, is formed inside the austenite grain by spinodal decomposition, which leads to a homogeneous distribution in the γ-matrix with a high dispersion (2–3 nm). However, in some works,[40,46,47] the spinodal character of the formation of κ-carbides is denied due to the discrepancy in the type of crystal lattice of the formed κ-carbide phase. The authors claim that κ-carbides with a simple cubic (SC) lattice are formed by coagulation of inclusions of the intermediate metastable phase L1_2, enriched in carbon.

claim: An increase in Mn content slows down the formation of intragranular κ-carbide by filling vacancies in the L1_2 volume-centered lattice instead of carbon atoms during redistribution.
evidence_text: An increase in the Mn content slows down the formation of intragranular κ-carbide due to the filling of vacancies in the volume-centered lattice L1_2 instead of carbon atoms during the redistribution of the latter.[8]

claim: A coherent interface between austenite and κ-carbide with a calculated lattice parameter mismatch of 2.7% along [001]κ//[001]γ results in low elastic strain energy, enabling spinodal decomposition at low undercooling to form nanoscale κ-carbide inclusions, whereas a high mismatch increases elastic strain energy and impairs mechanical properties.
evidence_text: The interface between austenite and κ-carbide is coherent, with a calculated lattice parameter mismatch of 2.7% along [001]κ//[0011]γ. The low discrepancy in lattice parameters between κ-carbide and austenite provides optimal mechanical properties and results in low elastic strain energy, which acts as a barrier to first-order phase transitions. Consequently, the possibility of spinodal decomposition arises at low degrees of undercooling, leading to the formation of nanoscale inclusions.

claim: Excessive silicon (>1–2 mass%) in Fe–Mn–Al–C steel promotes intergranular κ-carbide precipitation and red scale defects during hot rolling, so Si content is preferably limited to 1–2 mass%.
evidence_text: However, excessive silicon content in Fe–Mn–Al–C steel leads to the precipitation of intergranular κ-carbide, negatively affecting the properties of the lightweight steel. High silicon levels can result in the red scale defect during hot rolling... Therefore, it is preferable to limit the silicon content in lightweight steels to 1–2 mass%.[28]

claim: In Fe–30Mn–9Al–1.2C steel, without Si, ordered L1₂ nanodomains (<1 nm) form; with 2 mass% Si, cuboidal intragranular κ-carbides L'1₂ (11.5 nm, volume fraction 25.9%) and intergranular κ (50–200 nm) appear, raising yield strength from ≈450 MPa to ≈950 MPa and increasing strain hardening from 20% to 83%.
evidence_text: In silicon-free thermally treated steel, ordered L1 nanodomains (with a size of <1 nm) with a uniform distribution of elements were observed, while increasing the Si content to 2 mass% resulted in cuboidal intragranular κ-carbides L’1 with an average size of 11.5 nm and a volume fraction of 25.9%, along with well-developed element segregation. In the steel with 2 mass% Si, in addition to the coarsening of intragranular κ-carbides, significantly larger intergranular κ-carbides (50–200 nm) were found... Additionally, at the austenite grain boundaries in the Fe–Mn–Al–C steel with 2 mass% Si, the precipitation of κ-carbides inside the grains leads to an increase in yield strength from ≈450 MPa to ≈950 MPa, while the strain hardening due to the formation of localized coarse slip bands increases from 20% (at 0 mass% Si) to 55% and 83% at 1 mass% and 2 mass% Si, respectively.[46]

claim: In Fe–20Mn–9Al–3Cr–xC steel strips produced by centrifugal casting, increasing C content from 0.8 to 1.4 wt% reduces the volume fraction of ferrite from 36 vol% to 4 vol% and decreases the precipitation of the DO3 phase in ferrite.
evidence_text: J. Zhang et al. in their work[62] investigated ... produced using the centrifugal casting method. It was found that as the C content increased from 0.8 wt% to 1.4 wt%, the volume fraction of ferrite decreased from 36 vol% to 4 vol%, and the precipitation of the DO3 phase in the ferrite decreased.

claim: Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel, processed by optimized hot rolling and heat treatment, possesses a homogeneous austenite microstructure with fine κ-carbides and a very small fraction of ordered DO3 phase.
evidence_text: K. Kim et al.[66] successfully developed an ultralight steel Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr, which has a homogeneous austenite microstructure with fine κ-carbides within the austenite and a very small fraction of the ordered DO3 phase

claim: Increasing Al content to 13.2 mass% allowed a density reduction of approximately 20%, resulting in a steel density of 6.3 g·cm⁻³ for Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr.
evidence_text: Increasing the Al content to 13.2 mass% allowed for a reduction in the density of the Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel by ≈20%, resulting in a steel density of 6.3 g·cm−3 .

claim: At Cr content above 5 mass%, Cr-rich M7C3 carbides precipitate and the ordered DO3 phase fraction increases; tensile strength improves but elongation sharply decreases due to the high brittleness of the carbides.
evidence_text: At a Cr content of more than 5 mass%, Cr-rich M7C3 carbides precipitate, and the fraction of the ordered DO3 phase increases. While the tensile strength improves, the elongation sharply decreases due to the high brittleness of the carbides.

claim: Vanadium addition alone in Fe–27Mn–8Al–1.0C steel after annealing and aging enables dual VC and κ-carbide nanoprecipitation with tensile strength >1000 MPa and elongation ≈41%.
evidence_text: Furthermore, vanadium addition alone was shown to enable dual nanoprecipitation of VC and fine κ-carbides in Fe–27Mn–8Al–1.0C steels after annealing and aging, resulting in high tensile strengths (>1000 MPa) and good elongation (≈41%).

claim: In Fe–Mn–Al–C steel with 1.0 mass% V, cold rolling 20%, annealing at 900°C, and aging at 450–550°C produce co-precipitation of intragrain κ-carbides and V4C3, achieving tensile strength of 1300 MPa and specific strength of 190 MPa cm³ g⁻¹.
evidence_text: Intragrain κ-carbides and $\mathrm { V } _ { 4 } \mathrm { C } _ { 3 }$ particles can coprecipitate in the steel after cold rolling (20%), high-temperature annealing $( 9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } )$ , and low-temperature aging $( 4 5 0 – 5 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } )$ , resulting in an increase in tensile strength and specific strength of the austenitic steel with the addition of 1.0 mass% V to 1300 MPa and $1 9 0 { \mathrm { M P a c m } } ^ { 3 } { \mathrm { g } } ^ { - 1 } { \mathrm { , } }$ respectively.

claim: After cold rolling (20%), high-temperature annealing at 900°C, and low-temperature aging at 450–550°C, intragrain κ-carbides and V4C3 particles coprecipitate in Fe–Mn–Al–C steel with 1.0 mass% V, increasing tensile strength to 1300 MPa and specific strength to 190 MPa·cm³·g⁻¹.
evidence_text: Intragrain κ-carbides and V4C3 particles can coprecipitate in the steel after cold rolling (20%), high-temperature annealing (900°C), and low-temperature aging (450–550°C), resulting in an increase in tensile strength and specific strength of the austenitic steel with the addition of 1.0 mass% V to 1300 MPa and 190 MPa cm³ g⁻¹, respectively.

claim: From the perspective of enhancing strength, V is more promising than Nb.
evidence_text: From the perspective of enhancing strength, V is more promising than Nb.

claim: In cold-rolled Fe–28Mn–10Al–C steel, 0.5 wt% Nb alloying leads to significant grain refinement and improved mechanical characteristics, with ultimate tensile strength exceeding 900 MPa and elongation rising from ≈38% to 44% after solution treatment at 1050°C.
evidence_text: Recent studies have shown that 0.5 wt% Nb alloying in cold-rolled Fe–28Mn– 10Al–C steel results in significant grain refinement and improved mechanical characteristics, with ultimate tensile strength exceeding 900 MPa and elongation rising from ≈38% to 44% after solution treatment at 1050°C.

claim: In cold-rolled austenitic Fe–30Mn–5Al–1C–xMo steels (0–3 wt% Mo), Mo dissolves in austenite and precipitates Mo2C carbides during annealing, significantly delaying recrystallization and refining grains; alloys with ≥0.5 wt% Mo exhibit an ≈100°C increase in recrystallization temperature, along with increased hardness and yield strength.
evidence_text: Research[78] on cold-rolled austenitic Fe–30Mn–5Al–1C–xMo steels (0–3 wt% Mo) showed that Mo dissolves in austenite and precipitates Mo2C carbides during annealing, which significantly delayed recrystallization and led to grain refinement. Alloys with ≥0.5 wt% Mo exhibited ≈100 °C increase in recrystallization temperature, alongside increased hardness and yield strength.

claim: Mo2C precipitates substitute for a portion of κ-carbides, modifying precipitation behavior and improving strain-hardening characteristics; at 3 wt% Mo, yield strength increases by ≈36% and UTS by ≈18%, albeit with some ductility reduction.
evidence_text: Additionally, these Mo2C precipitates were observed to substitute for a portion of κ-carbides, thus modifying the precipitation behavior and improving strain-hardening characteristics yield strength increased by ≈36% and UTS by ≈18% at 3 wt% Mo, albeit with some ductility reduction.

claim: The addition of molybdenum exceeding 0.5 mass% allows for an increase in the recrystallization temperature by ≈100°C, widening the temperature range for hot deformation processing.
evidence_text: The addition of molybdenum exceeding 0.5 mass% allows for an increase in the recrystallization temperature by ≈100 °C, which widens the temperature range for hot deformation processing and is critically important for establishing optimal processing technology conditions for the steel.

claim: Fe–Mn–Al–C steel is sensitive to phosphorus content; phosphorus removal can be achieved through intensive calcium treatment and argon stirring, and the addition of mischmetal (<1 wt%) also neutralizes the effect of phosphorus.
evidence_text: Alloyed Fe–Mn–Al–C steel is sensitive to phosphorus content, so the melt and steel production technology must ensure protection from any contamination by phosphorus. Phosphorus removal can be achieved through intensive calcium treatment and argon stirring. The addition of small amounts of mischmetal (<1 wt%) also neutralizes the effect of phosphorus.

claim: Typically, a forged ingot of Fe–Mn–Al–C steel is homogenized at 1100–1250 °C for 1–3 h, followed by hot rolling to a thickness of 2–5 mm between 1100 and 850 °C (Figure 8).
evidence_text: Typically, a forged ingot is homogenized at 1100–1250 °C for 1–3 h, followed by hot rolling to achieve a thickness of 2–5 mm between 1100 and 850 °C ( ).Figure 8

claim: Fe–30Mn–9Al–1Si–0.9C–0.5Mo steel produced by induction-vacuum melting, hot rolling to 10.4 mm, water quenching, and aging at 530 °C for 10 h achieved hardness 340 HB, density 13% lower than rolled homogeneous armor (RHA), strength 10% higher, ballistic mass efficiency 1.1, and volumetric efficiency 1.02, enabling 13% weight reduction when replacing RHA directly.
evidence_text: This alloy was obtained using induction-vacuum melting followed by deformation hardening. The ingot was subjected to hot rolling to a thickness of 10.4 mm and quenched in water. The plate was then held at 530 °C for 10 h, achieving a hardness of 340 HB. ... The established density was 13% lower than that of rolled homogeneous armor (RHA), with strength characteristics 10% higher. ... Based on mass equivalence, the processed material achieved a mass efficiency of 1.1, while the volumetric equivalent efficiency was 1.02.
